attributes.stem:
Three days after undergoing a hip replacement, a 71-year-old woman shows signs of confusion. The nurse informs you that, eight hours after the procedure, the patient became tachycardic, hypertensive, and nauseous, but the symptoms improved spontaneously. Up until now, she had been comfortable and had been walking easily with a physiotherapist. Currently, the patient is agitated and says she can see spiders on the floor and rainbow coloured ghouls on the ceiling that are telling her that they are going to eat her; she has also been vomiting.

Her history includes major depression disorder, and chronic liver disease. She takes amitriptyline to help with her depression, and is adhering well. She drinks alcohol heavily, at approximately 5-10 beers a day. She has no known allergies.

On examination, she appears agitated, sweaty and tremulous. Her observations are blood pressure 121/94 mmHg, pulse 77/min, temperature 37.6 °C, respiratory rate 14/min and SpO2 96% on room air. She has dry mucous membranes, and nystagmus.

Her blood tests show:

  • Haemoglobin: 124g/L (130 – 180)

  • WCC 10.2x109/L (3.6 – 11.0)

  • Urea 19mmol/L (2.5 – 7.8)

  • Creatinine 90μmol/ L (45–84)

  • BSL 5.1mmol/L (4.0 to 6.0 mmol/L)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her current problem?
attributes.explanation:
Based on the information provided, this is most likely to be a case of delirium. The acute agitation and signs of confusion in this patient are characteristic signs of delirium. Individuals who recently undergo surgery, particularly older individuals undergoing a joint replacement, are at an elevated risk of developing delirium.

The cause of the patient's delirium is most likely to be alcohol withdrawal. This patient has a significant history of alcohol usage, which greatly increases the risk of alcohol withdrawal and subsequent delirium. Prior to the development of delirium, initial withdrawal symptoms such as tachycardia, hypertension, tremulousness, anxiety, nausea/vomiting, headache and diaphoresis may be seen, and some were seen in our patient. Several days later, the delirium developed in our patient - which is the trajectory of alcohol withdrawal. It is important to denote that this is a medical emergency, and alcohol withdrawal is a major cause of morbidity and mortality. The Assessment Scale for Alcohol (CIWA-Ar) is a system used to determine the presence and severity of alcohol withdrawal; symptoms include the following:

  • nausea and vomiting

  • tremor

  • paroxysmal sweats

  • anxiety

  • agitation

  • tactile disturbance

  • auditory disturbance

  • visual disturbance

  • headache, fullness in head

  • orientation and clouding of sensorium

While all of the other options are important causes of post-operative delirium, they are all less likely in this situation

Hypoxaemia - this is a low partial pressure of oxygen is not present here as the O2 sats in this patient are 96%.

Urinary tract infection - while this is an important cause of delirium, this patient's WCC is in the normal range and the patient is afebrile, making this less likely.

Depression - while depression can be a contributor, it is stated that the depression has been well treated with amitriptyline and is adhering as required.

Pain - while severe pain can be a contributor to the development of delirium and can concurrently lead to issues such as atelectasis, it was denoted that the patient was previously comfortable and walking well, therefore indicating that pain was not a major issue in this patient.
learningPoints: Patients who are acutely unwell may show symptoms of confusion that can be indicative of delirium.
For individuals with addiction, alcohol withdrawal is an important cause of delirium.
references: Garpestad, E., & Devlin, J. W. (2017). Polypharmacy and delirium in critically ill older adults: recognition and prevention. Clinics in geriatric medicine, 33(2), 189-203.
Torres‐Lockhart, K. E., Lu, T. Y., Weimer, M. B., Stein, M. R., & Cunningham, C. O. (2022). Clinical management of opioid withdrawal. Addiction, 117(9), 2540-2550.

Newman RK, Stobart Gallagher MA, Gomez AE. Alcohol Withdrawal. [Updated 2022 Aug 29]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441882/
Long, D., Long, B., & Koyfman, A. (2017). The emergency medicine management of severe alcohol withdrawal. The American journal of emergency medicine, 35(7), 1005-1011.
Grover, S., & Ghosh, A. (2018). Delirium tremens: assessment and management. Journal of clinical and experimental hepatology, 8(4), 460-470.
Hughes et al 2020 American Society for Enhanced Recovery and Perioperative Quality Initiative Joint Consensus Statement on Postoperative Delirium Prevention https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32022748/
Curtis et al 2020 Postoperative delirium: why, what, and how to confront it at your institution https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32796170/
Jin et al 2020 Postoperative delirium: perioperative assessment, risk reduction, and management https://www.bjanaesthesia.org/article/S0007-0912(20)30566-3/fulltext
